_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). General Medical: Anaphylaxis and Allergic Reaction. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Anaphylaxis and Allergic Reaction

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Anaphylaxis and Allergic Reaction
points: Provide timely therapy for potentially life-threatening reactions to known or suspected allergens to prevent cardiorespiratory collapse and shock
Provide symptomatic relief for symptoms due to known or suspected allergens

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients of all ages with suspected allergic reaction and/or anaphylaxis
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Remove or stop offending agent if possible
Epinephrine IM auto-injector 0.3-0.5 mg (adult) or 0.15 mg (pediatric <30 kg) into anterolateral mid-thigh
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98%
Supine position with legs elevated; semi-recumbent if respiratory distress
CPR/AED if pulseless anaphylactic shock develops
Prepare for airway management — anaphylaxis can cause rapid airway edema

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Epinephrine IM agency-stocked 0.3-0.5 mg (adult) q 5-15 min as needed
Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 25-50 mg IM/IV/IO for antihistamine effect
Albuterol 2.5 mg SVN for bronchospasm component
CPAP for severe bronchospasm component
12-lead ECG acquisition
IV/IO maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: Epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) IM if auto-injector not available
IV/IO access initiation; IV NS bolus 1-2 L (adult) or 20 mL/kg (pediatric) for hypotension
IV/IO epinephrine for cardiovascular collapse: 0.1-0.5 mg IV/IO (or dilute to 0.1 mg/mL)
IV ondansetron (Zofran) 4 mg for nausea/vomiting
IV diphenhydramine 50 mg for antihistamine effect
Waveform capnography

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Epinephrine infusion: 2-10 mcg/min IV for refractory anaphylactic shock (titrate to MAP >65 mmHg)
Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV (reduce late-phase reaction; does not affect acute response)
H1 and H2 blocker combination: diphenhydramine + ranitidine or famotidine
Glucagon 1-2 mg IV/IO for anaphylaxis in beta-blocker patients (bypasses beta receptor)
RSI and intubation for impending airway loss from angioedema — early preferred over late
Surgical airway (cricothyrotomy) if intubation impossible due to glottic edema
12-lead ECG interpretation for dysrhythmia
Vasopressors (norepinephrine, dopamine) for refractory hypotension

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Time to epinephrine delivery
Concentration of epinephrine in relation to route
Weight-based dosing of medications

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: When anaphylaxis is suspected, EMS personnel should always consider epinephrine as first-line treatment

Allergic reactions and anaphylaxis are serious and potentially life-threatening medical emergencies. It is the body's adverse reaction to a foreign protein (e.g., food, medicine, pollen, insect sting or any ingested, inhaled, or injected substance). A localized allergic reaction (e.g., urticaria or angioedema that does not compromise the airway) may be treated with antihistamine therapy. Cardiovascular collapse may occur abruptly, without the prior development of skin or respiratory symptoms. Constant monitoring of the patient's airway and breathing is essential

Contrary to common belief that all cases of anaphylaxis present with cutaneous manifestations, such as urticaria or mucocutaneous swelling, a significant portion of anaphylactic episodes may not involve these signs and symptoms on initial presentation. Moreover, most fatal reactions to food-induced anaphylaxis in children were not associated with cutaneous manifestations
A thorough assessment and a high index of suspicion are required for all potential allergic reaction patients ' consider: a. History of Present Illness i. Onset and location

Gastrointestinal symptoms occur most commonly in food-induced anaphylaxis, but can occur with other causes a. Oral pruritus is often the first symptom observed in patients experiencing food-induced anaphylaxis
Patients with asthma are at high-risk for a severe allergic reaction
There is no proven benefit to using steroids in the management of allergic reactions and/or anaphylaxis

There is controversy among experts with very low-quality evidence to guide management for the use of empiric IM epinephrine after exposure to a known allergen in asymptomatic patients with a history of prior anaphylaxis

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Medications given
' Dose and concentration of epinephrine given
' Route of epinephrine administration
' Time of epinephrine administration
' Signs and symptoms of the patient

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
